title: Nirvana vs Things 3 for Power users
meta_description: See why Things 3 fails first when context-based filtering and review workflow depth are constrained and why Nirvana is the better pick for power users.
constraint_lens: You run a strict GTD workflow and need granular context filters and structured review depth.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for power users implementing a strict GTD system.
one_second_verdict.reason: Things 3 fails first because context-based filtering and review workflow depth are constrained.

verdict.summary: Nirvana wins for power users implementing a strict GTD system. It includes dedicated lists for Next, Waiting, and Scheduled, plus context-based filtering built into the model. Things 3 supports areas, projects, and tags but does not enforce deep GTD stages or review layers. If context-based filtering and review workflow depth are constrained, Things 3 fails first.
verdict.decision_rule: If context-based filtering and review workflow depth are constrained, Things 3 fails first.

sections.heading: Why Nirvana fits Power users better
sections.content: Nirvana fits this power user because the winning mechanism reduces friction across setup, daily use, and organization rather than solving only one narrow problem.

bullets.point: Nirvana lowers the initial friction in a meaningful way
bullets.why_it_matters: The task tool becomes useful sooner instead of asking for structure that has not earned its place yet.

bullets.point: Nirvana keeps daily task handling faster
bullets.why_it_matters: The core workflow demands fewer steps and less second-guessing during routine use.

bullets.point: Nirvana organizes work in a way that stays understandable
bullets.why_it_matters: The structure supports the job instead of becoming another layer to manage.

bullets.point: Things 3 can still be better in a simpler setup
bullets.why_it_matters: The losing tool may remain the calmer option if the rule's friction is not showing up very often yet.

bullets.point: Things 3 may feel lighter for users who do not need the winner's depth
bullets.why_it_matters: Some workflows benefit more from a narrower surface than from extra capability.

bullets.point: Things 3 can reduce commitment up front
bullets.why_it_matters: That matters when the user is not ready to pay the cost of a more structured system.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: Nirvana becomes unnecessary when the workflow stays simpler than the verdict assumes.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose Things 3 if the lighter option is genuinely enough.

items.fails_when: Things 3 breaks down when the same named friction keeps recurring during setup, capture, and organization.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose Nirvana when that friction has become the actual bottleneck.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This can flip if the work stays simpler than the main verdict assumes. Then Things 3 may be easier without creating meaningful downsides.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Choose Nirvana when the friction named in the rule is already shaping daily use.
Choose Things 3 when the lighter surface is still enough.
Avoid Things 3 once the same friction keeps repeating across setup and execution.

faqs.q: Why does Nirvana fit the rule better?
faqs.a: Nirvana turns the same core mechanism into practical gains across setup, daily workflow, and organization instead of only in one narrow spot.
Things 3 is still reasonable when the friction in the decision rule is not showing up often enough to justify the winner's extra depth.

faqs.q: What usually signals it is time to switch?
faqs.a: The same friction starts appearing in several parts of the workflow rather than staying a one-off annoyance.

faqs.q: Is this verdict only about simplicity?
faqs.a: No. It is about where the real cost of using the system appears once the workflow becomes routine.
